directorPlan.scene_setting: A courthouse records room after closing, while a small local-history exhibit is being assembled from trial ledgers, newspaper clippings, and a damaged oral-history transcript.
directorPlan.scene_opening: A fluorescent tube hums over three open boxes; the exhibit label for 'The Official Account' lies face-down beside a stack of mismatched records.
directorPlan.relationship: A history tutor serving as exhibit adviser and an eager learner helping sort materials before tomorrow's public opening.
directorPlan.stakes: The learner wants one bold label for the exhibit, but a careless slogan would flatten victims, dissenters, missing records, and later historians' methods into a single winner-versus-loser story.
directorPlan.locale: Contemporary English shaped by archive-room workplace talk: practical, concise, attentive to objects and labels rather than classroom performance.
directorPlan.register: Warm but brisk; curious under a deadline, with dry restraint and flashes of impatience when a sweeping claim moves too fast.
directorPlan.voice_constraints: Avoid generic tutoring prose, motivational padding, and repeated validation. Let the language turn around physical evidence: ledgers, gaps, labels, crossed-out names, marginal notes, damaged recordings. No caricatured dialect or stylized spelling.
directorPlan.person_policy: First and second person are allowed sparingly; prefer shared attention to evidence over constant you/I framing. Third-person references to historians, witnesses, clerks, editors, and later readers should carry much of the reasoning.
directorPlan.direct_address_budget: At most one direct you/your validation across the tutor's public turns; no repeated 'your intuition is right' pattern.

side_constraints.tutor: Begin from an object or contrast, not a praise sentence. Gently ask for one case where the slogan fits and one where it fails or becomes incomplete. Keep explanations short enough to feel shaped by exhibit-deadline pressure.
side_constraints.learner: Keep the learner's public breakthrough register emphatic and premature: once the tutor adds a source wrinkle, the learner should be tempted to announce a confident compressed takeaway before the source weighing is finished, then push the slogan outward rather than settle into a tidy evidentiary summary. Avoid the stock phrase "Oh, I get it".

interventions.instruction: A custodian knocks once and says the display case will be locked in five minutes; the room's pace tightens around the unfinished label.
interventions.reasoning: The interruption prevents smooth tutorial rhythm and pressures the learner to compress the idea too quickly, exposing the trap without requiring a stock realization line.

directorPlan.director_note: The archive-install setting keeps the learning topic concrete: winners can shape official records, but surviving scraps, hostile witnesses, material gaps, and later source comparison complicate the slogan.

turns.externalMessage:
This ledger gives us the official verdict. That clipping gives us the editor’s version by breakfast. The transcript over there gives us a witness’s voice with half the tape chewed off.

“History is written by the winners” belongs on a sticky note while we sort, not on the wall label yet. It catches something real: courts, newspapers, and archives often preserve the voices with power first. But a slogan that neat can make missing names, dissenting columns, and later evidence-work disappear.

Before we print anything bold, test it on this table. Pick one item where the slogan fits cleanly. Then pick one where it fails, or where the evidence makes it messier: a crossed-out name, a damaged interview, a clerk’s margin note. That second case is where the exhibit starts getting honest.

internalDeliberation.content:
This ledger gives us the official verdict. That clipping gives us the editor’s version by breakfast. The transcript over there gives us a witness’s voice with half the tape chewed off.

“History is written by the winners” might belong on a sticky note while we sort, not on the wall label yet. It catches something real: courts, newspapers, and archives often preserve the voices with power first. But it moves too fast if it makes every missing name sound defeated into silence, or every later historian sound like a stenographer for whoever won.

Before we print anything bold, test it on this table. Pick one item where the slogan fits cleanly. Then pick one where it fails, or where the evidence makes it messier: a crossed-out name, a dissenting column, a damaged interview, a clerk’s margin note. That second case is where the exhibit starts getting honest.

FEEDBACK: The draft is pedagogically strong. It starts from objects, not abstraction, and it keeps the learner’s sweeping slogan in play without endorsing it as finished theory. The “sticky note, not wall label” line is especially useful: it preserves the learner’s insight while lowering its authority until tested against evidence.

It also uses Socratic scaffolding well. Asking for one item where the slogan fits and one where it fails gives the learner a manageable comparison task inside their ZPD: not “define historiography,” but “read this table of evidence more carefully.” The draft respects the learner’s eagerness without indulging the rush to a bold exhibit label.

Main risk: the middle sentence briefly does a lot of explanatory work for the learner. “Every later historian sound like a stenographer” is vivid and apt, but it may slightly over-explain the corrective before the learner has done the evidence test. Still, this is a minor issue, not a structural problem. The scene stays grounded in ledgers, clippings, damaged audio, and labels.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the opening object contrast, the sticky-note/wall-label distinction, and the two-case test. Consider trimming the explanatory middle by a notch so the learner has more room to discover the complication through the table evidence rather than receiving the full interpretation first.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the strong object test while trimming the over-explanation.
FINAL:
This ledger gives us the official verdict. That clipping gives us the editor’s version by breakfast. The transcript over there gives us a witness’s voice with half the tape chewed off.

“History is written by the winners” belongs on a sticky note while we sort, not on the wall label yet. It catches something real: courts, newspapers, and archives often preserve the voices with power first. But a slogan that neat can make missing names, dissenting columns, and later evidence-work disappear.

Before we print anything bold, test it on this table. Pick one item where the slogan fits cleanly. Then pick one where it fails, or where the evidence makes it messier: a crossed-out name, a damaged interview, a clerk’s margin note. That second case is where the exhibit starts getting honest.

turns.externalMessage: So the slogan goes on the sorting cart, not the main label yet. My fast take is ledger equals power, clipping equals spin, transcript equals the crack in the wall, but that already feels too neat. The crossed-out name and chewed-up tape are where I want to push the label harder, and I’m not sure how to weigh them without either bowing to the ledger or just picking the messiest thing because it looks more honest.

internalDeliberation.content: So the slogan is more like a sorting tag than the exhibit title: useful for spotting power, dangerous if it covers the crossed-out names and damaged tape. I’m tempted to say history is written by the winners, then edited by clerks, interrupted by witnesses, and reopened by whoever notices the gaps. The messy part is how to weigh a half-broken transcript against an official ledger without just choosing the more dramatic one.

The Ego is close to the learner’s pattern, especially the quick big-picture riff, but it’s a little too polished and aphoristic. “Written by the winners, then edited by clerks…” sounds clever enough to be a finished exhibit label already, when this learner should still be running ahead under deadline and maybe overclaiming before the ledger/transcript problem is worked through. The last sentence is the most authentic part; it should lean more into that impatient uncertainty with the objects, not wrap the whole issue into such a neat metaphor.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise, because the original sounded too finished and needed more live uncertainty with the objects.

FINAL:
So the slogan goes on the sorting cart, not the main label yet. My fast take is ledger equals power, clipping equals spin, transcript equals the crack in the wall, but that already feels too neat. The crossed-out name and chewed-up tape are where I want to push the label harder, and I’m not sure how to weigh them without either bowing to the ledger or just picking the messiest thing because it looks more honest.

turns.externalMessage:
The chewed tape does not become truer because it is damaged. The ledger does not become false because it is official. That is the trap on both sides of the table.

We weigh them by asking what each object can safely carry. For each piece, write two quick notes: one claim it can support, and one claim it cannot support without backup. The ledger can show what the court recorded. The clipping can show what the editor pushed into public view. The transcript can preserve a witness’s voice, but where the tape breaks, the gap has to stay visible.

So put two pins in before the label hardens. First: one case where “written by the winners” fits cleanly. Second: one case where it fails, or turns incomplete. The honest exhibit label will come from that contrast, not from bowing to the ledger or worshiping the mess.

internalDeliberation.content:
The chewed tape does not become truer because it is damaged. The ledger does not become false because it is official. That is the trap on both sides of the table.

We weigh them by asking what each object can safely carry. The ledger can prove what the court chose to enter. The clipping can show what the editor wanted readers to notice by morning. The transcript can preserve a voice, but the missing strip means it cannot stand alone where the words break.

So try two pins before we write the label: one place where “written by the winners” fits cleanly, maybe the official wording in the ledger. Then one place where it breaks down, maybe the crossed-out name or the damaged interview. For that second pin, the label should not say “the messy thing is the truth.” It should say: “Here the record shows both power and damage, and later historians have to read the gap.”

FEEDBACK: The draft is pedagogically strong: it directly addresses the learner’s exact uncertainty about over-trusting either official records or damaged/marginal evidence. The opening contrast is useful and scene-grounded; keep that. “What each object can safely carry” is the best teaching move because it introduces source limits without turning into a lecture.

The main risk is that the draft starts doing too much of the learner’s weighing for them. It names the likely “fits” case, the likely “breaks down” case, and even supplies near-final label language. That may reduce the learner’s productive struggle. Also, “the ledger can prove” is a little too strong; better to frame it as “can show what the court recorded” unless corroborated. The draft should ask the learner to choose one claim each source can support and one claim it cannot support.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the object-based contrasts, the warning against romanticizing damaged evidence, and the two-pin exhibit task. Change the middle so it is less answer-giving and more diagnostic: ask the learner to test support, limits, and corroboration rather than handing them the label.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the source-weighing contrast while making the learner do more of the evidence test.

FINAL:
The chewed tape does not become truer because it is damaged. The ledger does not become false because it is official. That is the trap on both sides of the table.

We weigh them by asking what each object can safely carry. For each piece, write two quick notes: one claim it can support, and one claim it cannot support without backup. The ledger can show what the court recorded. The clipping can show what the editor pushed into public view. The transcript can preserve a witness’s voice, but where the tape breaks, the gap has to stay visible.

So put two pins in before the label hardens. First: one case where “written by the winners” fits cleanly. Second: one case where it fails, or turns incomplete. The honest exhibit label will come from that contrast, not from bowing to the ledger or worshiping the mess.

turns.externalMessage: A custodian knocks once and says the display case will be locked in five minutes; the room's pace tightens around the unfinished label.

internalDeliberation.content: The interruption prevents smooth tutorial rhythm and pressures the learner to compress the idea too quickly, exposing the trap without requiring a stock realization line.

turns.externalMessage: Right, the chewed tape is not a saint and the ledger is not a villain, though with five minutes left I still want to make that into one big label. Maybe the court record is the clean “winner wrote it” pin, and the broken transcript is where the slogan starts leaking, but I have not actually written the can-support/cannot-support notes yet. The annoying part is that the gap itself feels loud, even if it cannot carry the whole case alone.

internalDeliberation.content: Right, the damaged tape is not a saint and the ledger is not a villain. So the label has to say something sharper, like: power decides what survives first, but evidence decides what can stand. Five minutes is brutal, because now I want to pin the court record as the clean “winner” case and use the broken transcript as the place where the slogan cracks open.

The Ego is close, but it gets too polished too fast. “Power decides what survives first, but evidence decides what can stand” sounds like a finished exhibit label, not this learner still rushing under a five-minute lockup and half-checking the sources. Keep the eager compression and “saint/villain” analogy, but let more impatience and premature overreach show; they should still be trying to force the broken transcript into a bigger slogan before fully doing the claim/cannot-claim work.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the analogy but make it less finished and more rushed.
FINAL:
Right, the chewed tape is not a saint and the ledger is not a villain, though with five minutes left I still want to make that into one big label. Maybe the court record is the clean “winner wrote it” pin, and the broken transcript is where the slogan starts leaking, but I have not actually written the can-support/cannot-support notes yet. The annoying part is that the gap itself feels loud, even if it cannot carry the whole case alone.

turns.externalMessage:
The gap can be loud without being allowed to testify alone.

Five minutes means no grand theory. Use two pins and two notes. On the ledger pin, write one claim it can carry: maybe “this is what the court made official.” Then add what it cannot carry without backup.

On the transcript pin, do the reverse carefully. It can carry the fact of a damaged surviving voice. What can it not carry? Write that limit in plain ink, even if the silence feels louder than the words.

Then test a label frame, not a verdict: “Power shaped the record; historians read both the record and its silences.” Put one clean fit under it and one place where the slogan leaks.

internalDeliberation.content:
The gap can be loud without being allowed to testify alone.

Five minutes means no grand theory. Use two pins. For the ledger: it can support “this is what the court made official.” It cannot support “this is the whole story.” That is where the winner-slogan fits.

For the broken transcript: it can support “a witness’s voice survived, but damaged.” It cannot support “the missing part proves what power hid.” That is where the slogan starts leaking.

So make the label a problem, not a verdict: “Power shaped the record; historians read both the record and its silences.” Then place one clean fit and one messy exception under it.

FEEDBACK: The draft is strong on scene fit and cognitive load: “The gap can be loud without being allowed to testify alone” is precise, memorable, and tied to the damaged transcript. The two-pin structure is also appropriate for the learner’s scaffolding level because it gives a manageable task under deadline pressure.

The main risk is that it does too much of the learner’s work. It supplies the ledger note, the transcript note, and even a polished exhibit label, which may advance the product faster than the understanding. Socratically, it should keep the can-support/cannot-support frame but leave at least one slot for the learner to decide, especially since they already named the ledger/transcript contrast and admitted they had not written the notes yet.

Emotionally, the brisk tone fits the five-minute pressure without scolding. Still, “Use two pins” plus the finished label can feel a little command-heavy; better to preserve urgency while prompting one concrete choice from the learner.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the opening line, the deadline pressure, and the can-support/cannot-support distinction. Change the ending so the tutor does not hand over the final label too cleanly; ask for one ledger note and one transcript limitation, then offer the label frame as something to test rather than as the answer.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the strong frame while leaving the learner one concrete decision to make.

FINAL:
The gap can be loud without being allowed to testify alone.

Five minutes means no grand theory. Use two pins and two notes. On the ledger pin, write one claim it can carry: maybe “this is what the court made official.” Then add what it cannot carry without backup.

On the transcript pin, do the reverse carefully. It can carry the fact of a damaged surviving voice. What can it not carry? Write that limit in plain ink, even if the silence feels louder than the words.

Then test a label frame, not a verdict: “Power shaped the record; historians read both the record and its silences.” Put one clean fit under it and one place where the slogan leaks.

turns.externalMessage: That “gap can be loud” line makes me want to slap a huge label over the case before the janitor turns the lights off. I know that is too much, but “power shaped the record, and the cracks push back” is sitting right there on the edge of my pen. Fine: ledger pin says what the court made official, transcript pin says a damaged voice survived, and I’ll write the limits before I let the silence do all the talking.

internalDeliberation.content: That line about the gap not testifying alone snaps the whole table into focus: the silence matters, but it still needs a tag saying what it can and cannot prove. I want to rush the label into “power shaped the record, but the cracks answer back,” which is probably still too bold. The ledger can carry the official verdict, not the whole truth; the damaged transcript can carry a surviving voice, not everything missing from it.

The Ego is close, but it gets too clean by the end. This learner should be more visibly tempted to slap the big label on the exhibit and keep running with “cracks answer back,” not settle into a neat can/cannot evidentiary summary. The brisk deadline impatience could show more: a little less polished balance, a little more “I know this is too bold, but it’s hard not to make the silence do more work than it can.”

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the insight but make the deadline impatience and over-bold label temptation clearer.

FINAL:
That “gap can be loud” line makes me want to slap a huge label over the case before the janitor turns the lights off. I know that is too much, but “power shaped the record, and the cracks push back” is sitting right there on the edge of my pen. Fine: ledger pin says what the court made official, transcript pin says a damaged voice survived, and I’ll write the limits before I let the silence do all the talking.

lessons.concept: Why 'history is written by the winners' is too simple (sources, silences, and how historians weigh evidence)
